subtopic_detail: Group B Streptococcus positive labour: intrapartum prophylaxis
stem: A 30-year-old woman in her first pregnancy presents at 39 weeks gestation in spontaneous labour with intact membranes. Her routine vaginal-rectal swab at 36 weeks returned positive for Group B Streptococcus. She has no penicillin allergy. She is now 5 cm dilated with regular contractions and a reassuring cardiotocograph. Her vital signs are normal.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, intact membranes, cephalic presentation, normal CTG
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks positive; FBC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum management?

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 600 mg eight hourly as standard prophylaxis option without susceptibility data
options.rationale: Clindamycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with susceptible GBS isolates given resistance rates. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily until delivery as appropriate prophylaxis cover
options.rationale: Oral prophylaxis does not achieve adequate intrapartum tissue and fetal levels for GBS disease prevention. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 20 mg/kg six hourly without renal monitoring throughout the labour period
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy or known macrolide resistance and is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: No antibiotics now as routine intrapartum prophylaxis is not recommended for routine GBS positive
options.rationale: Routine intrapartum prophylaxis for GBS positive women is the AU standard of care for prevention of early-onset GBS sepsis. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal GBS in pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g four hourly until delivery occurs
options.rationale: AU intrapartum GBS prophylaxis is intravenous benzylpenicillin from the onset of labour until delivery to prevent early-onset GBS disease in the newborn. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal GBS in pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Maternal Group B Streptococcus colonisation affects approximately 15 to 25% of pregnant women. AU practice screens with combined vaginal-rectal swab at 35 to 37 weeks gestation. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is given to women who are GBS positive at screening, those with GBS bacteriuria in the current pregnancy, those with a previous infant with invasive GBS disease, or those with unknown status and risk factors at labour. First-line is intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g four hourly. Cefazolin is used for non-severe penicillin allergy; clindamycin or vancomycin for severe allergy depending on susceptibility.
key_points: GBS positive screening at 35 to 37 weeks indicates intrapartum benzylpenicillin
Cefazolin replaces benzylpenicillin in non-severe penicillin allergy
Vancomycin or susceptibility-guided clindamycin applies to severe penicillin allergy
explanation.pearls: Adequate intrapartum prophylaxis requires the first dose at least 4 hours before delivery; shorter intervals reduce protection against early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis.

subtopic_detail: Cervical screening HSIL result and colposcopy pathway
stem: A 32-year-old woman attends to discuss her latest cervical screening result. She had a routine self-collected cervical screening test, which returned positive for HPV-16. Reflex liquid-based cytology shows high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion. She is asymptomatic, sexually active in a monogamous relationship, and has never had an abnormal screen previously. She is not pregnant and has a copper IUD in place.

data_table.Examination: Speculum examination normal at routine follow up appointment
data_table.Investigations: HPV-16 positive; reflex LBC HSIL; pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat cervical screening test in 6 months with referral only if HPV remains positive
options.rationale: Higher risk results require direct referral to colposcopy, not deferred repeat testing. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat the HPV cervical screening test in 12 months without colposcopy referral first
options.rationale: 12 month repeat applies to intermediate risk results, not higher risk with HSIL cytology. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Remove the copper IUD before any further investigation can be safely performed today
options.rationale: Copper IUD does not interfere with colposcopy or cervical biopsy and need not be removed. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Empirical large loop excision of the transformation zone without prior colposcopy
options.rationale: Treatment without colposcopic assessment can over-treat and is not supported by AU guidelines. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for colposcopy within 2 weeks for directed biopsy and treatment planning
options.rationale: Higher risk HPV with HSIL cytology mandates colposcopy within 2 weeks per AU NCSP pathways. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The Australian National Cervical Screening Program uses 5 yearly HPV primary screening with reflex liquid-based cytology if higher risk types are detected. HPV 16 or 18 positive plus HSIL or worse on cytology is a higher risk result and is referred for colposcopy within 2 weeks. HPV non-16/18 positive with low-grade cytology is intermediate risk with 12 month repeat HPV test. Self-collection is approved for women aged 25 to 74 and gives equivalent sensitivity for high-risk HPV. Treatment of biopsy-proven HSIL is by excisional therapy (LLETZ) or ablative therapy, with surveillance HPV test at 12 months post-treatment.
key_points: Higher risk HPV with HSIL cytology requires colposcopy within 2 weeks
Intermediate risk (HPV non-16/18 with low-grade cytology) is rechecked at 12 months
Self-collected HPV testing is approved for women aged 25 to 74 with equivalent sensitivity
explanation.pearls: Self-collection cervical screening returns the same higher risk pathway as clinician-collected; reflex cytology is performed on the same sample when HPV 16 or 18 positive.

subtopic_detail: Recurrent pregnancy loss workup at three losses
stem: A 34-year-old woman attends gynaecology with her partner after a third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage. All losses were spontaneous and confirmed on ultrasound. There is no history of stillbirth or second trimester loss. She has regular menstrual cycles, BMI 24, and no medical conditions. She does not smoke or drink. The partner is well and 36 years old. The previous loss had products of conception that were not karyotyped.

data_table.Examination: Pelvic examination normal, no abnormal bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Previous TVUS showed normal uterine cavity; FBC, TSH, fasting glucose all normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial workup?

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibody panel plus parental karyotype and pelvic cavity imaging assessment
options.rationale: AU and international guidance for recurrent pregnancy loss screens for antiphospholipid syndrome, uterine anomalies and parental karyotype as the priority initial workup. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Empirical heparin and aspirin in next pregnancy with no investigations performed today now
options.rationale: Empirical heparin without diagnosed antiphospholipid syndrome lacks evidence in unexplained recurrent miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia screen for factor V Leiden and prothrombin gene mutations only
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia screening is no longer part of the routine recurrent miscarriage workup outside specific situations. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Pre-implantation genetic testing for aneuploidy referral for the next attempt without other tests
options.rationale: PGT-A may be considered after structural and parental karyotype workup, not as the sole initial step. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Endometrial biopsy in the secretory phase to screen for chronic endometritis alone
options.rationale: Chronic endometritis testing is selective and does not replace the standard antiphospholipid plus structural plus karyotype workup. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

explanation.summary: Recurrent pregnancy loss is defined as three or more consecutive miscarriages, with workup also reasonable after two losses with concerning history. Established causes include antiphospholipid syndrome (treated with low-dose aspirin and prophylactic LMWH), uterine anomalies, parental balanced translocations, untreated thyroid disease, and uncontrolled diabetes. Standard initial workup includes antiphospholipid antibodies on two occasions 12 weeks apart, pelvic imaging (3D ultrasound, saline infusion sonohysterography, or MRI), parental karyotype, thyroid function, and HbA1c. Around 50% of recurrent miscarriages have no identified cause, and expectant pregnancy management has a 60 to 70% live birth rate.
key_points: Antiphospholipid antibody screen is a priority because treatment alters outcome
Uterine cavity assessment (3D ultrasound, SIS, or MRI) identifies treatable anomalies
Parental karyotype is selective in women under 39 without family history but standard after three losses
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid syndrome diagnosis requires two positive antibody results 12 weeks apart; a single positive result is not enough to label and treat.

subtopic_detail: Operative vaginal delivery: vacuum versus forceps selection
stem: A 30-year-old primigravida is in the second stage of labour at 40 weeks gestation. She has been pushing for 90 minutes with adequate contractions. The fetal head is at +2 station in direct occipito-anterior position with no caput or moulding. The cardiotocograph shows late decelerations with reduced variability. She has an effective epidural in situ. The obstetric registrar is supervised by an experienced consultant in the labour ward.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 116/74, HR 96, T 36.8; fetal CTG late decelerations, baseline 145, reduced variability
data_table.Examination: Fully dilated cervix, head at +2 station, direct OA position, no caput, no moulding, intact perineum
data_table.Investigations: Bedside ultrasound confirms OA position; bedside fetal scalp lactate 4.0 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Category 1 caesarean section in theatre transferring immediately for delivery within 30 minutes
options.rationale: Category 1 caesarean is reserved when vaginal delivery is not safely achievable; outlet vacuum delivery is preferable when feasible. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Vacuum-assisted vaginal delivery using a Kiwi cup with appropriate analgesia in situ
options.rationale: Outlet operative vaginal delivery with vacuum is appropriate at +2 OA station with non-reassuring CTG and adequate analgesia. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Continue active pushing for a further 30 minutes with maternal repositioning techniques first
options.rationale: Continued pushing in the face of category 2 fetal heart rate trace at +2 station with prolonged second stage delays definitive intervention. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Apply intrauterine pressure catheter to titrate oxytocin and increase the contraction frequency
options.rationale: Increasing oxytocin in the face of late decelerations and fetal compromise risks worsening fetal acidosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

options.text: Mid-cavity rotational forceps without prior vacuum attempt as primary instrument selection
options.rationale: Rotational forceps are reserved for malposition or operator preference; OA position at +2 favours vacuum first in AU practice. (Source: RANZCOG: Instrumental vaginal birth)

explanation.summary: Operative vaginal delivery is indicated for fetal compromise, maternal exhaustion, or prolonged second stage. Pre-requisites include full cervical dilation, ruptured membranes, identified fetal position, head at or below the ischial spines, adequate analgesia, empty bladder, consent, and ability to convert to caesarean section. Vacuum is generally preferred for outlet and low-cavity deliveries because of lower maternal trauma and equivalent fetal outcomes when used appropriately. Forceps are favoured for mid-cavity rotation, prematurity under 34 weeks, face presentation, or after-coming head. A failed vacuum should not be followed by sequential forceps in the absence of compelling reasons because of compounded neonatal injury risk.
key_points: Vacuum extraction has lower maternal trauma than forceps for outlet delivery
Pre-requisites include full dilation, identified position, station at or below spines, analgesia, empty bladder, consent, and theatre access
Sequential vacuum then forceps increases neonatal injury and is avoided where possible
explanation.pearls: Vacuum should not be used below 34 weeks gestation because of increased neonatal subgaleal haemorrhage and intraventricular haemorrhage risk.

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia at 36 weeks: timing of delivery
stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks gestation is admitted with blood pressure 168/108 mmHg, 3+ proteinuria on dipstick, and headache. Bloods show platelets 92 x10^9/L, ALT 80 U/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, and urate 0.42 mmol/L. The cardiotocograph is reassuring with baseline 140 and normal variability. She has had a single dose of nifedipine slow release 20 mg with BP reduction to 152/96 and started intravenous magnesium sulfate. She has had a course of corticosteroids 4 days ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 152/96 after nifedipine, HR 86, T 36.9, RR 18, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes, no clonus, no visual disturbance now, headache persisting; CTG reassuring
data_table.Investigations: Plt 92 x10^9/L, ALT 80 U/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, urine PCR 320 mg/mmol

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Expectant management with daily monitoring and aim to reach 39 weeks gestation for delivery
options.rationale: Continued expectant management with multi-organ involvement risks maternal eclampsia, abruption, and fetal compromise. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Delivery is recommended now given severe pre-eclampsia with end-organ involvement at 36 weeks
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia at or beyond 34 to 37 weeks with end-organ features (thrombocytopenia, transaminitis) is an indication for delivery. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Continue magnesium sulfate for 48 hours and reassess for delivery once severity has stabilised
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is given for seizure prophylaxis around delivery, not as a means to delay it. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Repeat course of corticosteroids today and plan delivery in 48 hours after second course completes
options.rationale: Repeat steroid courses at this gestation are not indicated and delivery should not be delayed for further steroids at 36 weeks. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Outpatient management with daily blood pressure monitoring and twice weekly bloods until 38 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient management is inadequate for severe pre-eclampsia at 36 weeks with thrombocytopenia and transaminitis. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia is hypertension with proteinuria or end-organ involvement after 20 weeks gestation. Severe disease is defined by blood pressure 160/110 mmHg or higher, proteinuria, or end-organ features (thrombocytopenia, transaminitis, renal impairment, pulmonary oedema, cerebral or visual disturbance, severe epigastric pain). Antihypertensive control to systolic 140 to 155 mmHg and diastolic 90 to 105 mmHg, intravenous magnesium sulfate for severe disease and to prevent eclampsia, and corticosteroids if under 34 weeks are key. Delivery is indicated immediately for severe disease 34 to 37 weeks, eclampsia, abruption, deteriorating end-organ function, or non-reassuring fetal status.
key_points: Severe pre-eclampsia at or beyond 34 to 37 weeks is an indication for delivery
BP target is 140 to 155 / 90 to 105 mmHg in pregnancy
Magnesium sulfate prevents progression to eclampsia and is loaded then run as infusion
explanation.pearls: Magnesium sulfate loading is 4 g IV over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour infusion, continued for 24 hours after delivery or last seizure with monitoring of reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output.
